title: Die kluge Else
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 34. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: The thirty-fourth tale in the Grimms’ collection (KHM 34), a comedy of foolishness dressed as cleverness. Sent to the cellar to draw beer, Else spies a pickaxe on the wall and weeps over a child she has not yet borne, killed by a fall that will never happen — and one by one the whole household comes down and weeps with her, until Hans marries her on the strength of such ‘sense.’ Later, netted with little bells as she sleeps in the field instead of reaping, she wakes so bewildered that she no longer knows whether she is Else, and, told at her own door that Else is already inside, runs out of the village forever. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as daß, gieng, fieng, ward, erschrack, thun/thäte/that, todt, todtgeschlagen, heirathen, nöthig, Hausthüre and gescheidt.

sentences.t: Es war ein Mann, der hatte eine Tochter, die hieß die kluge Else.
sentences.l: There was a man, who had a daughter, who was called Clever Else.
sentences.i: There was once a man who had a daughter, and she was called Clever Else.
sentences.n: The title is already the joke: “die kluge Else,” Clever Else — a name the tale spends every page disproving. The relative “der hatte” resumes the man with a demonstrative der (‘he had’), a very Grimm way of pointing back to a noun.

sentences.t: Als sie nun erwachsen war, sprach der Vater „wir wollen sie heirathen lassen.“
sentences.l: When she now was grown up, the father spoke, “we want to let her marry.”
sentences.i: When she had grown up, the father said, “Let us have her married.”
sentences.n: “heirathen” carries the old -th- (modern heiraten, to marry); you will meet this th throughout the edition (thun, Thür, Thränen). “wir wollen … lassen” = ‘let us have (her) married.’

sentences.t: „Ja,“ sagte die Mutter, „wenn nur einer käme, der sie haben wollte.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” said the mother, “if only one would come, who wanted to have her.”
sentences.i: “Yes,” said the mother, “if only someone would come who wanted her.”
sentences.n: “käme” and “wollte” are subjunctive II — the wished-for, not-yet-real suitor. “einer” = ‘someone, a man.’

sentences.t: Endlich kam von weither einer, der hieß Hans, und hielt um sie an, er machte aber die Bedingung, daß die kluge Else auch recht gescheidt wäre.
sentences.l: At last there came from afar one, who was called Hans, and asked for her hand; but he made the condition that Clever Else be really sensible too.
sentences.i: At last a man came from far away, called Hans, and asked for her hand; but he made it a condition that Clever Else really be sensible.
sentences.n: “hielt um sie an” is the separable verb anhalten (um jemanden), the set phrase for formally asking for a woman’s hand. “gescheidt” is the 1857 spelling of gescheit (clever, sensible). “wäre” is subjunctive of the reported condition — and the whole tale hangs on whether she is in fact gescheidt.

sentences.t: „O,“ sprach der Vater, „die hat Zwirn im Kopf,“ und die Mutter sagte „ach, die sieht den Wind auf der Gasse laufen und hört die Fliegen husten.“
sentences.l: “Oh,” spoke the father, “she has thread in her head,” and the mother said, “ah, she sees the wind run down the lane and hears the flies cough.”
sentences.i: “Oh,” said the father, “she has a good head on her,” and the mother said, “why, she can see the wind running down the street and hear the flies coughing.”
sentences.n: The parents’ praise is pure comic hyperbole. “Zwirn im Kopf haben” (literally ‘to have thread in one’s head’) is a folk phrase for being sharp-witted — see the glossary word Zwirn. Seeing the wind run and hearing flies cough are absurd feats of keenness; the reader is meant to smile, because everything that follows shows the opposite.

sentences.t: „Ja,“ sprach der Hans, „wenn sie nicht recht gescheidt ist, so nehm ich sie nicht.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” spoke Hans, “if she is not really sensible, then I take her not.”
sentences.i: “Yes,” said Hans, “if she isn’t truly sensible, then I won’t have her.”
sentences.n: “nehm” is the 1857 clipped form of nehme (I take). “der Hans” — Grimm often puts the definite article before a personal name, a colloquial, folksy touch (‘the Hans’).

sentences.t: Als sie nun zu Tisch saßen und gegessen hatten, sprach die Mutter „Else, geh in den Keller und hol Bier.“
sentences.l: When they now sat at table and had eaten, the mother spoke, “Else, go into the cellar and fetch beer.”
sentences.i: When they were sitting at table and had eaten, the mother said, “Else, go down to the cellar and fetch some beer.”
sentences.n: “zu Tisch sitzen” = to sit at table. geh and hol are bare imperatives (modern would keep them the same). This errand sets the whole comedy going.

sentences.t: Da nahm die kluge Else den Krug von der Wand, gieng in den Keller und klappte unterwegs brav mit dem Deckel, damit ihr die Zeit ja nicht lang würde.
sentences.l: Then Clever Else took the jug from the wall, went into the cellar and clattered along the way heartily with the lid, so that the time would not grow long for her.
sentences.i: Then Clever Else took the jug down from the wall, went to the cellar, and clacked away busily with the lid on the way, so that the time should not hang heavy on her.
sentences.n: “gieng” is the 1857 spelling of ging (went); the old -ie- recurs (fieng, anfieng). “brav” here doesn’t mean ‘well-behaved’ but ‘heartily, with a will.’ “damit ihr die Zeit nicht lang würde” — ‘so that time should not drag for her’ (jemandem wird die Zeit lang, an impersonal dative idiom). Clacking a lid to pass a two-minute errand is our first real glimpse of her ‘cleverness.’

sentences.t: Als sie unten war, holte sie ein Stühlchen, und stellte es vors Faß, damit sie sich nicht zu bücken brauchte und ihrem Rücken etwa nicht wehe thäte und unverhofften Schaden nähme.
sentences.l: When she was down below, she fetched a little stool and set it in front of the cask, so that she need not stoop and might not perhaps hurt her back and take unexpected harm.
sentences.i: When she was down below, she fetched a little stool and set it in front of the cask, so that she needn’t stoop and might not somehow hurt her back and come to unexpected harm.
sentences.n: The elaborate care she takes against imagined harm is the same over-thinking that will produce the pickaxe fear. “vors” = vor das (in front of the). “wehe thäte” — weh thun = to hurt (thäte is the subjunctive of thun, modern täte); “Schaden nehmen” = to come to harm. Note the dative of possession “ihrem Rücken … wehe thäte,” literally ‘hurt to her back.’

sentences.t: Dann stellte sie die Kanne vor sich und drehte den Hahn auf, und während der Zeit daß das Bier hinein lief, wollte sie doch ihre Augen nicht müßig lassen, sah oben an die Wand hinauf und erblickte nach vielem Hin- und Herschauen eine Kreuzhacke gerade über sich, welche die Maurer da aus Versehen hatten stecken lassen.
sentences.l: Then she set the can before her and turned the tap open, and during the time that the beer ran in, she would not leave her eyes idle after all, looked up at the wall and, after much looking to and fro, caught sight of a pickaxe right above her, which the masons had left stuck there by mistake.
sentences.i: Then she set the jug before her and turned on the tap, and while the beer was running in she would not let her eyes be idle, but looked up the wall and, after much looking this way and that, spied a pickaxe right over her head, which the masons had left stuck there by mistake.
sentences.n: “drehte … auf” (aufdrehen, to turn on) is separable. “Hin- und Herschauen” is a nominalized ‘looking to and fro.’ “stecken lassen” = to leave (something) stuck; here the double infinitive “hatten stecken lassen” (had left stuck). The pickaxe overhead is the little seed the whole absurd avalanche grows from.

sentences.t: Da fieng die kluge Else an zu weinen und sprach „wenn ich den Hans kriege, und wir kriegen ein Kind, und das ist groß, und wir schicken das Kind in den Keller, daß es hier soll Bier zapfen, so fällt ihm die Kreuzhacke auf den Kopf und schlägts todt.“
sentences.l: Then Clever Else began to weep and spoke, “if I get Hans, and we get a child, and it is big, and we send the child into the cellar to tap beer here, then the pickaxe falls on its head and strikes it dead.”
sentences.i: Then Clever Else began to weep and said, “If I marry Hans, and we have a child, and it grows big, and we send the child down to the cellar to draw beer here, then the pickaxe will fall on its head and strike it dead.”
sentences.n: Here is the tale’s engine: a chain of ‘and … and … and’ conditions leaping from an unmarried girl to a dead future child, all from a pickaxe on a wall. “fieng … an” (anfangen, to begin) is separable, in the old spelling. “schlägts” = schlägt es (‘strikes it [dead]’); “todt” is the 1857 spelling of tot (dead). The comedy is that she weeps over a grief she has entirely invented.

sentences.t: Da saß sie und weinte und schrie aus Leibeskräften über das bevorstehende Unglück.
sentences.l: There she sat and wept and screamed with all her might over the impending misfortune.
sentences.i: And there she sat and wept and screamed at the top of her lungs over the coming disaster.
sentences.n: “aus Leibeskräften” = with all one’s bodily strength, at the top of one’s lungs. “das bevorstehende Unglück” — the ‘impending’ misfortune that exists only in her head.

sentences.t: Die oben warteten auf den Trank, aber die kluge Else kam immer nicht.
sentences.l: The ones above waited for the drink, but Clever Else still did not come.
sentences.i: The people upstairs waited for the drink, but Clever Else did not come at all.
sentences.n: “Die oben” = ‘those above / upstairs’ (the article used as a noun). “kam immer nicht” — ‘still would not come’ (immer here = ‘the whole while, still’).

sentences.t: Da sprach die Frau zur Magd „geh doch hinunter in den Keller und sieh wo die Else bleibt.“
sentences.l: Then the woman spoke to the maid, “do go down into the cellar and see where Else is getting to.”
sentences.i: Then the wife said to the maid, “Do go down to the cellar and see what’s keeping Else.”
sentences.n: “wo … bleibt” — ‘where she is staying / getting to,’ i.e. what is keeping her (a set idiom, ‘wo bleibt er?’ = ‘where has he got to?’). “doch” is a softening, urging particle.

sentences.t: Die Magd gieng und fand sie vor dem Fasse sitzend und laut schreiend.
sentences.l: The maid went and found her sitting before the cask and loudly screaming.
sentences.i: The maid went and found her sitting before the cask, screaming aloud.
sentences.n: “Fasse” has the old dative -e (das Faß). “sitzend” and “schreiend” are present participles used as adverbs — ‘sitting … and screaming.’

sentences.t: „Else, was weinst du?“ fragte die Magd.
sentences.l: “Else, what do you weep for?” asked the maid.
sentences.i: “Else, what are you crying about?” asked the maid.
sentences.n: “weinst” is the 2nd person singular of weinen; “was weinst du?” = ‘what are you weeping about?’

sentences.t: „Ach,“ antwortete sie „soll ich nicht weinen? wenn ich den Hans kriege, und wir kriegen ein Kind, und das ist groß, und soll hier Trinken zapfen, so fällt ihm vielleicht die Kreuzhacke auf den Kopf und schlägt es todt.“
sentences.l: “Ah,” she answered, “should I not weep? if I get Hans, and we get a child, and it is big, and is to tap drink here, then perhaps the pickaxe falls on its head and strikes it dead.”
sentences.i: “Ah,” she answered, “how could I not weep? If I marry Hans, and we have a child, and it grows big and is to draw drink here, then the pickaxe may fall on its head and strike it dead.”
sentences.n: She recites her disaster a second time, almost word for word — the tale, like her, repeats itself. “Trinken” here is a noun, ‘something to drink,’ standing in for the beer (elsewhere she says Bier). Each retelling recruits another weeper.

sentences.t: Da sprach die Magd „was haben wir für eine kluge Else!“ setzte sich zu ihr und fieng auch an über das Unglück zu weinen.
sentences.l: Then the maid spoke, “what a clever Else we have!” sat herself down beside her and also began to weep over the misfortune.
sentences.i: Then the maid said, “What a clever Else we have!” sat down beside her, and also began to weep over the misfortune.
sentences.n: “was haben wir für eine kluge Else!” is the tale’s refrain — spoken by each newcomer in mock-admiration, the irony thickening every time. “was für ein …” = ‘what a …’. Note that no one questions the fear; they simply join in.

sentences.t: Über eine Weile, als die Magd nicht wiederkam, und die droben durstig nach dem Trank waren, sprach der Mann zum Knecht „geh doch hinunter in den Keller und sieh wo die Else und die Magd bleibt.“
sentences.l: After a while, when the maid did not come back, and those up above were thirsty for the drink, the man spoke to the farmhand, “do go down into the cellar and see where Else and the maid are getting to.”
sentences.i: After a while, when the maid did not come back and those upstairs were thirsty for their drink, the master said to the farmhand, “Do go down to the cellar and see what’s keeping Else and the maid.”
sentences.n: “wiederkam” = came back (wiederkommen, separable). “die droben” = ‘those up above’ (droben, up there, up above). The comic pattern now sends a third person down the stairs.

sentences.t: Der Knecht gieng hinab, da saß die kluge Else und die Magd, und weinten beide zusammen.
sentences.l: The farmhand went down; there sat Clever Else and the maid, and both wept together.
sentences.i: The farmhand went down; there sat Clever Else and the maid, weeping together.
sentences.n: “gieng hinab” (hinabgehen, to go down) is separable. der Knecht is a hired manservant or farmhand, the male counterpart to die Magd.

sentences.t: Da fragte er „was weint ihr denn?“
sentences.l: Then he asked, “what are you weeping for, then?”
sentences.i: Then he asked, “Why, what are you crying about?”
sentences.n: “denn” here is the flavouring particle of a genuine question, ‘what on earth …?’. “ihr” is the plural ‘you’ — he addresses both women.

sentences.t: „Ach,“ sprach die Else, „soll ich nicht weinen? wenn ich den Hans kriege, und wir kriegen ein Kind, und das ist groß, und soll hier Trinken zapfen, so fällt ihm die Kreuzhacke auf den Kopf, und schlägts todt.“
sentences.l: “Ah,” spoke Else, “should I not weep? if I get Hans, and we get a child, and it is big, and is to tap drink here, then the pickaxe falls on its head, and strikes it dead.”
sentences.i: “Ah,” said Else, “how could I not weep? If I marry Hans, and we have a child, and it grows big and is to draw drink here, then the pickaxe will fall on its head and strike it dead.”
sentences.n: The identical lament a third time — the deliberate repetition is the joke. “schlägts” again = schlägt es.

sentences.t: Da sprach der Knecht „was haben wir für eine kluge Else!“ setzte sich zu ihr und fieng auch an laut zu heulen.
sentences.l: Then the farmhand spoke, “what a clever Else we have!” sat himself down beside her and also began to howl loudly.
sentences.i: Then the farmhand said, “What a clever Else we have!” sat down beside her, and also began to bawl aloud.
sentences.n: The refrain again, now from the farmhand. “heulen” = to howl, bawl — a notch louder than weinen; the group grief keeps escalating.

sentences.t: Oben warteten sie auf den Knecht, als er aber immer nicht kam, sprach der Mann zur Frau „geh doch hinunter in den Keller und sieh wo die Else bleibt.“
sentences.l: Above they waited for the farmhand, but when he still did not come, the man spoke to the woman, “do go down into the cellar and see where Else is getting to.”
sentences.i: Upstairs they waited for the farmhand, but when he still did not come, the master said to his wife, “Do go down to the cellar and see what’s keeping Else.”
sentences.n: The same errand, now sending the wife — the fourth to descend. The pattern is a folk-tale accumulation, each figure vanishing into the cellar in turn.

sentences.t: Die Frau gieng hinab und fand alle drei in Wehklagen, und fragte nach der Ursache, da erzählte ihr die Else auch daß ihr zukünftiges Kind wohl würde von der Kreuzhacke todtgeschlagen werden, wenn es erst groß wäre, und Bier zapfen sollte, und die Kreuzhacke fiele herab.
sentences.l: The woman went down and found all three in wailing, and asked after the cause; then Else told her too that her future child would surely be struck dead by the pickaxe, when it was once grown big, and was to tap beer, and the pickaxe fell down.
sentences.i: The wife went down and found all three lamenting, and asked the reason; whereupon Else told her too that her future child would surely be struck dead by the pickaxe, once it had grown big and was to draw beer and the pickaxe came down.
sentences.n: Now the narrator reports her fear in the subjunctive of reported speech: “würde … todtgeschlagen werden … wäre … sollte … fiele” — the whole imaginary catastrophe held at arm’s length in the subjunctive. “todtgeschlagen” is the 1857 spelling of totgeschlagen (struck dead); “fiele” is subjunctive II of fallen.

sentences.t: Da sprach die Mutter gleichfalls „ach, was haben wir für eine kluge Else!“ setzte sich hin und weinte mit.
sentences.l: Then the mother likewise spoke, “ah, what a clever Else we have!” sat herself down and wept along.
sentences.i: Then the mother said likewise, “Ah, what a clever Else we have!” sat down, and wept with the rest.
sentences.n: “gleichfalls” = likewise, in the same way. “weinte mit” (mitweinen, to weep along with the others) is separable — the mit- underlines that she simply joins the chorus.

sentences.t: Der Mann oben wartete noch ein Weilchen, als aber seine Frau nicht wieder kam, und sein Durst immer stärker ward, sprach er „ich muß nur selber in den Keller gehn und sehen wo die Else bleibt.“
sentences.l: The man above waited yet a little while, but when his wife did not come back, and his thirst grew ever stronger, he spoke, “I must just go into the cellar myself and see where Else is getting to.”
sentences.i: The master upstairs waited a little longer, but when his wife did not come back and his thirst grew stronger and stronger, he said, “I shall just have to go down to the cellar myself and see what’s keeping Else.”
sentences.n: “Weilchen” is the diminutive of Weile (a little while). “ward” is the archaic preterite of werden (became/grew); modern German uses wurde — you will meet ward often in Grimm. “gehn” is a clipped gehen. Even the last man standing is drawn down into the cellar of tears.

sentences.t: Als er aber in den Keller kam, und alle da bei einander saßen und weinten, und er die Ursache hörte, daß das Kind der Else schuld wäre, das sie vielleicht einmal zur Welt brächte, und von der Kreuzhacke könnte todtgeschlagen werden, wenn es gerade zur Zeit, wo sie herab fiele, darunter säße, Bier zu zapfen: da rief er „was für eine kluge Else!“ setzte sich und weinte auch mit.
sentences.l: But when he came into the cellar, and all sat there together and wept, and he heard the cause — that Else’s child was to blame, whom she might perhaps one day bring into the world, and who could be struck dead by the pickaxe, if just at the moment when it fell down it were sitting underneath to tap beer — then he cried, “what a clever Else!” sat down and also wept along.
sentences.i: But when he came into the cellar, and all of them sat there together weeping, and he heard the reason — that the culprit was Else’s child, whom she might one day bring into the world, and who could be struck dead by the pickaxe if it happened to be sitting underneath drawing beer just as the pickaxe fell — then he cried, “What a clever Else!” sat down, and wept along with the rest.
sentences.n: The longest sentence piles subjunctive on subjunctive (“wäre … brächte … könnte … fiele … säße”), stacking one hypothetical inside another until the not-yet-conceived child is solemnly declared ‘to blame.’ “zur Welt bringen” = to give birth to (brächte, subjunctive of bringen). With the father’s tears, the whole household weeps over nothing.

sentences.t: Der Bräutigam blieb lange oben allein, da niemand wiederkommen wollte, dachte er „sie werden unten auf dich warten, du mußt auch hingehen und sehen was sie vorhaben.“
sentences.l: The bridegroom stayed long above alone; since no one would come back, he thought, “they will be waiting for you down below, you must go there too and see what they are up to.”
sentences.i: The bridegroom stayed upstairs alone a long while; since no one would come back, he thought, “They must be waiting for you down there; you had better go down too and see what they are about.”
sentences.n: der Bräutigam is Hans, the suitor. “was sie vorhaben” — vorhaben (separable) = to intend, be up to. Hans talks himself down the stairs in the second person (‘you must go’) — a homely, thinking-aloud style.

sentences.t: Als er hinab kam, saßen da fünfe und schrien und jammerten ganz erbärmlich, einer immer besser als der andere.
sentences.l: When he came down, there sat five and screamed and wailed quite piteously, one always better than the other.
sentences.i: When he came down, there sat five of them, screaming and wailing most piteously, each one louder than the last.
sentences.n: “fünfe” is an old inflected form of fünf, ‘five of them.’ “jammerten” = wailed, lamented. “einer immer besser als der andere” — ‘each one outdoing the next,’ here comically: better at wailing.

sentences.t: „Was für ein Unglück ist denn geschehen?“ fragte er.
sentences.l: “What misfortune has then happened?” he asked.
sentences.i: “Why, what misfortune has happened?” he asked.
sentences.n: “was für ein …” = ‘what (kind of) …’. Hans, the one outsider to the panic, is the reader’s stand-in, asking the sane question.

sentences.t: „Ach, lieber Hans,“ sprach die Else, „wann wir einander heirathen und haben ein Kind, und es ist groß, und wir schickens vielleicht hierher Trinken zu zapfen, da kann ihm ja die Kreuzhacke, die da oben ist stecken geblieben, wenn sie herabfallen sollte, den Kopf zerschlagen, daß es liegen bleibt; sollen wir da nicht weinen?“
sentences.l: “Ah, dear Hans,” spoke Else, “when we marry one another and have a child, and it is big, and we send it here perhaps to tap drink, then the pickaxe, which has stayed stuck up there, can indeed, if it should fall down, smash its head, so that it lies there dead; should we not weep at that?”
sentences.i: “Ah, dear Hans,” said Else, “when we marry and have a child, and it grows big, and we send it here perhaps to draw drink, then the pickaxe stuck up there could, if it should fall, smash its head so that it lies there dead — should we not weep at that?”
sentences.n: “wann” here is used for wenn (‘when, if’). “schickens” = schicken es (‘send it’). “stecken geblieben” = stayed stuck (stecken bleiben); “zerschlagen” = to smash. She lays out the fantasy one last time — and it is precisely this that wins Hans over.

sentences.t: „Nun,“ sprach Hans, „mehr Verstand ist für meinen Haushalt nicht nöthig; weil du so eine kluge Else bist, so will ich dich haben,“ packte sie bei der Hand und nahm sie mit hinauf und hielt Hochzeit mit ihr.
sentences.l: “Well,” spoke Hans, “more sense is not needed for my household; since you are such a clever Else, I will have you,” grasped her by the hand and took her up with him and held a wedding with her.
sentences.i: “Well,” said Hans, “no more sense than that is needed for my household; since you are such a clever Else, I will have you,” and he took her by the hand, led her upstairs, and married her.
sentences.n: The irony lands: her wild foolishness is precisely what Hans calls Verstand (sense), and on that ‘proof’ of cleverness he marries her. “nöthig” carries the 1857 -th- (modern nötig). “Hochzeit halten” = to hold a wedding, to marry.

sentences.t: Als sie den Hans eine Weile hatte, sprach er „Frau, ich will ausgehen arbeiten und uns Geld verdienen, geh du ins Feld, und schneid das Korn, daß wir Brot haben.“
sentences.l: When she had had Hans a while, he spoke, “wife, I will go out to work and earn us money; you go into the field and cut the grain, so that we have bread.”
sentences.i: When she had been married to Hans a while, he said, “Wife, I am going out to work and earn us some money; you go into the field and reap the grain, so that we may have bread.”
sentences.n: “ausgehen” (to go out) is separable; “ausgehen arbeiten” = go out to work. “schneid das Korn” — schneiden here = to cut, i.e. to reap; das Korn is the standing grain crop. geh and schneid are bare imperatives.

sentences.t: „Ja, mein lieber Hans, das will ich thun.“
sentences.l: “Yes, my dear Hans, that I will do.”
sentences.n: “thun” is the 1857 spelling of tun (to do). Fronting the object “das” (‘that I will do’) throws the verb ahead of the subject — normal German once anything but the subject opens the clause.

sentences.t: Nachdem der Hans fort war, kochte sie sich einen guten Brei und nahm ihn mit ins Feld.
sentences.l: After Hans was gone, she cooked herself a good porridge and took it along into the field.
sentences.i: After Hans had gone, she cooked herself a good bowl of porridge and took it along to the field.
sentences.n: der Brei = porridge, pap, mush. “nahm … mit” (mitnehmen, to take along) is separable. Note the reflexive dative “sich … kochte,” ‘cooked for herself.’

sentences.t: Als sie vor den Acker kam, sprach sie zu sich selbst „was thu ich? schneid ich ehr, oder eß ich ehr? hei, ich will erst essen.“
sentences.l: When she came before the field, she spoke to herself, “what do I do? do I reap first, or do I eat first? hey, I will eat first.”
sentences.i: When she came to the field, she said to herself, “What shall I do? Shall I reap first, or eat first? Hey — I’ll eat first.”
sentences.n: der Acker is the ploughed field. “ehr” is an old form of eher (‘sooner, first’). “hei” is a little cry of resolve, ‘hey!’. This mock-deliberation — reap or eat, reap or sleep — is her ‘cleverness’ again: much weighing, no working.

sentences.t: Nun aß sie ihren Topf mit Brei aus, und als sie dick satt war, sprach sie wieder „was thu ich? schneid ich ehr, oder schlaf ich ehr? hei, ich will erst schlafen.“
sentences.l: Now she ate her pot of porridge out, and when she was fat full, she spoke again, “what do I do? do I reap first, or do I sleep first? hey, I will sleep first.”
sentences.i: So she ate up her whole pot of porridge, and when she was stuffed full, she said again, “What shall I do? Shall I reap first, or sleep first? Hey — I’ll sleep first.”
sentences.n: “aß … aus” (aufessen/ausessen, to eat up) is separable. “dick satt” = ‘fat full,’ i.e. stuffed. The same little debate as before, resolved the same lazy way.

sentences.t: Da legte sie sich ins Korn und schlief ein.
sentences.l: Then she laid herself in the grain and fell asleep.
sentences.i: Then she lay down in the grain and fell asleep.
sentences.n: “schlief ein” (einschlafen, to fall asleep) is separable; the ein- marks the moment of dropping off, as against schlafen (to be asleep).

sentences.t: Der Hans war längst zu Haus, aber die Else wollte nicht kommen, da sprach er „was hab ich für eine kluge Else, die ist so fleißig, daß sie nicht einmal nach Haus kommt und ißt.“
sentences.l: Hans was long since at home, but Else would not come; then he spoke, “what a clever Else I have — she is so diligent that she doesn’t even come home and eat.”
sentences.i: Hans had long been home, but Else would not come; so he said, “What a clever Else I have — so hard-working that she won’t even come home to eat.”
sentences.n: The refrain returns, now in Hans’s own mouth and doubly ironic: he praises as diligence exactly what is idleness (she is asleep, not reaping). “nicht einmal” = ‘not even.’

sentences.t: Als sie aber noch immer ausblieb und es Abend ward, gieng der Hans hinaus, und wollte sehen was sie geschnitten hätte: aber es war nichts geschnitten, sondern sie lag im Korn und schlief.
sentences.l: But when she still stayed away and it became evening, Hans went out and wanted to see what she had cut; but nothing was cut — instead she lay in the grain and slept.
sentences.i: But when she still did not come and evening fell, Hans went out to see what she had reaped; but nothing had been reaped — she lay in the grain, asleep.
sentences.n: “ausblieb” (ausbleiben, to stay away, fail to come) is separable. “geschnitten hätte” is subjunctive of reported expectation (what he supposed she had reaped); “nichts geschnitten” flatly contradicts it. “ward” again = became.

sentences.t: Da eilte Hans geschwind heim, und holte ein Vogelgarn mit kleinen Schellen und hängte es um sie herum; und sie schlief noch immer fort.
sentences.l: Then Hans hurried swiftly home, and fetched a fowling-net with little bells and hung it around her; and she slept on still.
sentences.i: Then Hans hurried quickly home, fetched a fowling-net hung with little bells, and draped it all around her; and still she slept on.
sentences.n: das Vogelgarn is a net for catching birds; the Schellen are the small jingle-bells on it. “hängte … um sie herum” — ‘hung around about her.’ “schlief … fort” (fortschlafen, to sleep on) is separable. This bird-net is the trap that will spring Else’s identity crisis.

sentences.t: Dann lief er heim, schloß die Hausthüre zu und setzte sich auf seinen Stuhl und arbeitete.
sentences.l: Then he ran home, shut the house-door to and sat himself on his chair and worked.
sentences.i: Then he ran home, locked the front door, sat down on his chair, and set to work.
sentences.n: “Hausthüre” carries the old -th- (modern Haustür). “schloß … zu” (zuschließen, to lock shut) is separable. Locking the door is what will leave Else, later, shut out of her own life.

sentences.t: Endlich, als es schon ganz dunkel war, erwachte die kluge Else, und als sie aufstand, rappelte es um sie herum, und die Schellen klingelten bei jedem Schritte, den sie that.
sentences.l: At last, when it was already quite dark, Clever Else awoke, and when she stood up, it rattled all around her, and the bells jingled at every step that she took.
sentences.i: At last, when it was already quite dark, Clever Else awoke, and when she stood up, there was a rattling all around her, and the bells jingled at every step she took.
sentences.n: “rappelte es” — the impersonal es with rappeln (to rattle, clatter): ‘there was a rattling.’ “klingelten” = jingled (klingeln). “Schritte” has the old dative -e (der Schritt); “den sie that” = ‘that she took’ (thun/tun, in the 1857 spelling). The jingling she cannot place is about to unseat her sense of who she is.

sentences.t: Da erschrack sie, ward irre ob sie auch wirklich die kluge Else wäre und sprach „bin ichs, oder bin ichs nicht?“
sentences.l: Then she took fright, grew uncertain whether she really were Clever Else after all, and spoke, “is it I, or is it not I?”
sentences.i: Then she was frightened, and grew unsure whether she really was Clever Else at all, and said, “Is it me, or is it not me?”
sentences.n: This is the tale’s famous turn. “erschrack” is the archaic preterite of erschrecken (took fright; modern erschrak). “irre werden” = to grow confused, lose one’s bearings; “ward irre” uses the archaic ward (became). “bin ichs, oder bin ichs nicht?” — literally ‘am I it, or am I not it?’: ichs = ich es. Rather than test her doubt, she will go and ask others to settle whether she is herself — the perfect final proof of her ‘cleverness.’

sentences.t: Sie wußte aber nicht was sie darauf antworten sollte und stand eine Zeitlang zweifelhaft: endlich dachte sie „ich will nach Haus gehen und fragen ob ichs bin oder ob ichs nicht bin, die werdens ja wissen.“
sentences.l: But she did not know what she should answer to that and stood a while in doubt; at last she thought, “I will go home and ask whether it is I or whether it is not I — they will surely know.”
sentences.i: But she did not know what to answer to that, and stood a while in doubt; at last she thought, “I’ll go home and ask whether it’s me or not — they’ll surely know.”
sentences.n: “wußte” is the 1857 spelling of wusste (knew). “zweifelhaft” = doubtful, uncertain. “die werdens ja wissen” — werdens = werden es; ja is the confident ‘surely.’ That she trusts ‘they’ to know who she is, rather than herself, is the joke driven home.

sentences.t: Sie lief vor ihre Hausthüre, aber die war verschlossen: da klopfte sie an das Fenster und rief „Hans, ist die Else drinnen?“
sentences.l: She ran up to her house-door, but it was locked; then she knocked on the window and called, “Hans, is Else inside?”
sentences.i: She ran to her own front door, but it was locked; so she knocked at the window and called, “Hans, is Else inside?”
sentences.n: She asks after herself in the third person — ‘is Else inside?’ — as though she were someone else entirely. “drinnen” = inside, in there. “die war verschlossen” — die points back to the door.

sentences.t: „Ja,“ antwortete der Hans, „sie ist drinnen.“
sentences.l: “Yes,” answered Hans, “she is inside.”
sentences.n: Hans’s calm ‘yes’ — whether he is playing along or has simply gone to bed assuming she is home — is what seals her conviction that she is not herself.

sentences.t: Da erschrack sie, und sprach „ach Gott, dann bin ichs nicht,“ und gieng vor eine andere Thür; als aber die Leute das Klingeln der Schellen hörten, wollten sie nicht aufmachen, und sie konnte nirgend unterkommen.
sentences.l: Then she took fright and spoke, “ah God, then it is not I,” and went to another door; but when the people heard the jingling of the bells, they would not open up, and she could find shelter nowhere.
sentences.i: Then she was frightened and said, “Oh God, then it isn’t me,” and went to another door; but when the people heard the jingling of the bells, they would not open, and she could find shelter nowhere.
sentences.n: The answer she trusted more than herself convinces her she is no one. “aufmachen” (to open up) is separable. “unterkommen” = to find shelter, a place to stay; “nirgend” is the older nirgends (nowhere). Now homeless in her own identity, she has nowhere left to go but away.

sentences.t: Da lief sie fort zum Dorfe hinaus, und niemand hat sie wieder gesehen.
sentences.l: Then she ran away out of the village, and no one has seen her again.
sentences.i: Then she ran off out of the village, and no one ever saw her again.
sentences.n: “lief … fort” (fortlaufen, to run away) is separable; “zum Dorfe hinaus” = out of the village (Dorfe with the old dative -e). The tale ends not with a lesson but a shrug: convinced by others that she is not herself, Clever Else simply runs out of the world of the story for good.
